title: RISC-V Processor Simulator
description: Build a cycle-accurate RISC-V RV32I processor simulator from scratch, progressively adding pipelining, caching, and branch prediction. The flagship project of the course.

milestones.title: RV32I Instruction Decoder and Single-Cycle Simulator
milestones.description: Decode all RV32I base integer instructions from raw 32-bit words and execute them on a single-cycle datapath. By the end of this milestone you will have a working RISC-V simulator capable of running real programs — Fibonacci, bubble sort, and string reversal — entirely in Python.
learningObjectives: Understand the RV32I instruction encoding formats: R, I, S, B, U, J
Implement sign extension for immediate fields of varying widths
Build a byte-addressable memory model with load/store support
Simulate a 32-register file with x0 hardwired to zero
Execute arithmetic, logical, shift, branch, jump, and memory instructions
Trace instruction execution and verify correctness against known outputs
milestones.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, List, Tuple
import struct

# =============================================================================
# RISC-V RV32I Processor Simulator — Milestone 1
# Single-Cycle Datapath
# =============================================================================

# --- Opcode Constants ---
OPCODE_R_TYPE    = 0b0110011   # Register-register ALU
OPCODE_I_TYPE    = 0b0010011   # Register-immediate ALU
OPCODE_LOAD      = 0b0000011   # Load from memory
OPCODE_STORE     = 0b0100011   # Store to memory
OPCODE_BRANCH    = 0b1100011   # Conditional branch
OPCODE_JAL       = 0b1101111   # Jump and link
OPCODE_JALR      = 0b1100111   # Jump and link register
OPCODE_LUI       = 0b0110111   # Load upper immediate
OPCODE_AUIPC     = 0b0010111   # Add upper immediate to PC
OPCODE_SYSTEM    = 0b1110011   # ECALL / EBREAK

# --- Funct3 for R-type and I-type ALU ---
FUNCT3_ADD_SUB   = 0b000
FUNCT3_SLL       = 0b001
FUNCT3_SLT       = 0b010
FUNCT3_SLTU      = 0b011
FUNCT3_XOR       = 0b100
FUNCT3_SRL_SRA   = 0b101
FUNCT3_OR        = 0b110
FUNCT3_AND       = 0b111

# --- Funct3 for Branch ---
FUNCT3_BEQ       = 0b000
FUNCT3_BNE       = 0b001
FUNCT3_BLT       = 0b100
FUNCT3_BGE       = 0b101
FUNCT3_BLTU      = 0b110
FUNCT3_BGEU      = 0b111

# --- Funct3 for Load/Store ---
FUNCT3_BYTE      = 0b000
FUNCT3_HALF      = 0b001
FUNCT3_WORD      = 0b010
FUNCT3_BYTE_U    = 0b100
FUNCT3_HALF_U    = 0b101


def sign_extend(value: int, bits: int) -> int:
    """Sign-extend a `bits`-wide integer to a full 32-bit signed int."""
    if value & (1 << (bits - 1)):
        value -= (1 << bits)
    return value


def to_unsigned_32(value: int) -> int:
    """Mask to 32-bit unsigned representation."""
    return value & 0xFFFFFFFF


def to_signed_32(value: int) -> int:
    """Interpret a 32-bit unsigned int as signed (two's complement)."""
    value = value & 0xFFFFFFFF
    if value & 0x80000000:
        return value - 0x100000000
    return value


@dataclass
class Instruction:
    """Decoded RISC-V instruction with all fields extracted."""
    raw: int                          # Original 32-bit instruction word
    opcode: int = 0
    rd: int = 0
    rs1: int = 0
    rs2: int = 0
    funct3: int = 0
    funct7: int = 0
    imm: int = 0                      # Sign-extended immediate
    fmt: str = ""                     # Format: 'R', 'I', 'S', 'B', 'U', 'J'

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        return (f"Instruction(fmt={self.fmt}, opcode=0b{self.opcode:07b}, "
                f"rd=x{self.rd}, rs1=x{self.rs1}, rs2=x{self.rs2}, "
                f"funct3=0b{self.funct3:03b}, funct7=0b{self.funct7:07b}, "
                f"imm={self.imm})")


class Memory:
    """Byte-addressable memory with configurable size.
    
    Memory is stored as a bytearray. All addresses are unsigned 32-bit.
    Supports byte, halfword, and word loads/stores in little-endian order.
    """

    def __init__(self, size: int = 1024 * 1024):  # 1 MB default
        self.data = bytearray(size)
        self.size = size

    def load_byte(self, addr: int) -> int:
        """Load an unsigned byte from memory."""
        addr = addr % self.size
        return self.data[addr]

    def load_half(self, addr: int) -> int:
        """Load an unsigned halfword (2 bytes, little-endian)."""
        addr = addr % self.size
        return self.data[addr] | (self.data[addr + 1] << 8)

    def load_word(self, addr: int) -> int:
        """Load an unsigned word (4 bytes, little-endian)."""
        addr = addr % self.size
        return (self.data[addr]
                | (self.data[addr + 1] << 8)
                | (self.data[addr + 2] << 16)
                | (self.data[addr + 3] << 24))

    def store_byte(self, addr: int, value: int) -> None:
        """Store a byte to memory."""
        addr = addr % self.size
        self.data[addr] = value & 0xFF

    def store_half(self, addr: int, value: int) -> None:
        """Store a halfword (2 bytes, little-endian)."""
        addr = addr % self.size
        self.data[addr] = value & 0xFF
        self.data[addr + 1] = (value >> 8) & 0xFF

    def store_word(self, addr: int, value: int) -> None:
        """Store a word (4 bytes, little-endian)."""
        addr = addr % self.size
        self.data[addr] = value & 0xFF
        self.data[addr + 1] = (value >> 8) & 0xFF
        self.data[addr + 2] = (value >> 16) & 0xFF
        self.data[addr + 3] = (value >> 24) & 0xFF

    def load_program(self, instructions: List[int], base_addr: int = 0) -> None:
        """Load a list of 32-bit instruction words into memory starting at base_addr."""
        for i, word in enumerate(instructions):
            self.store_word(base_addr + i * 4, word)


class RegisterFile:
    """32 general-purpose registers. x0 is hardwired to zero."""

    def __init__(self):
        self.regs = [0] * 32

    def read(self, reg: int) -> int:
        """Read register value. x0 always returns 0."""
        if reg == 0:
            return 0
        return to_unsigned_32(self.regs[reg])

    def write(self, reg: int, value: int) -> None:
        """Write value to register. Writes to x0 are silently ignored."""
        if reg != 0:
            self.regs[reg] = to_unsigned_32(value)

    def dump(self) -> str:
        """Return a formatted dump of all register values."""
        lines = []
        for i in range(0, 32, 4):
            parts = [f"x{i+j:2d}=0x{self.read(i+j):08x}" for j in range(4)]
            lines.append("  ".join(parts))
        return "\n".join(lines)


def decode(word: int) -> Instruction:

        B-type:  imm[12|10:5] | rs2[24:20] | rs1[19:15] | funct3[14:12] | imm[4:1|11]| opcode[6:0]
        U-type:  imm[31:12]                                              | rd[11:7] | opcode[6:0]
        J-type:  imm[20|10:1|11|19:12]                                   | rd[11:7] | opcode[6:0]

class CPU:
    """Single-cycle RISC-V RV32I processor.
    
    Executes one instruction per cycle: fetch -> decode -> execute.
    Maintains PC, register file, and memory.
    """

    def __init__(self, memory: Memory):
        self.memory = memory
        self.regs = RegisterFile()
        self.pc: int = 0
        self.cycle_count: int = 0
        self.instruction_count: int = 0
        self.halted: bool = False

    def fetch(self) -> int:
        """Fetch the 32-bit instruction word at the current PC."""
        word = self.memory.load_word(self.pc)
        return word

    def execute(self, inst: Instruction) -> None:
        """Execute a decoded instruction, updating registers, memory, and PC.
        
        TODO: Students implement this function.
        
        Handle all RV32I instructions:
          - R-type: ADD, SUB, SLL, SLT, SLTU, XOR, SRL, SRA, OR, AND
          - I-type ALU: ADDI, SLTI, SLTIU, XORI, ORI, ANDI, SLLI, SRLI, SRAI
          - Load: LB, LH, LW, LBU, LHU
          - Store: SB, SH, SW
          - Branch: BEQ, BNE, BLT, BGE, BLTU, BGEU
          - JAL, JALR
          - LUI, AUIPC
          - SYSTEM: ECALL (use as halt — set self.halted = True)
        
        Important:
          - Always advance PC by 4 unless a branch/jump changes it.
          - x0 must always remain 0 (RegisterFile.write handles this).
          - Use to_signed_32() for signed comparisons.
          - Use to_unsigned_32() to keep values in 32-bit range.
        """
        next_pc = self.pc + 4  # Default: advance to next instruction

        # TODO: Implement execution for each opcode/funct3/funct7 combination

        # Suggested structure:
        #   if inst.opcode == OPCODE_R_TYPE:
        #       if inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_ADD_SUB:
        #           if inst.funct7 == 0b0000000:  # ADD
        #               ...
        #           elif inst.funct7 == 0b0100000:  # SUB
        #               ...
        #       elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SLL:  ...
        #       ...
        #   elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_I_TYPE:  ...
        #   elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_LOAD:    ...
        #   ... etc.

        pass  # <-- Replace with your implementation

        self.pc = to_unsigned_32(next_pc)

    def step(self) -> Optional[Instruction]:
        """Execute a single instruction cycle: fetch -> decode -> execute."""
        if self.halted:
            return None

        word = self.fetch()
        if word == 0:
            self.halted = True
            return None

        inst = decode(word)
        self.execute(inst)
        self.cycle_count += 1
        self.instruction_count += 1
        return inst

    def run(self, max_cycles: int = 100000) -> int:
        """Run until halt or max_cycles. Returns total instructions executed."""
        while not self.halted and self.cycle_count < max_cycles:
            self.step()
        return self.instruction_count

    def get_cpi(self) -> float:
        """Cycles per instruction. For single-cycle, always 1.0."""
        if self.instruction_count == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.cycle_count / self.instruction_count
milestones.solutionCode:
def decode(word: int) -> Instruction:
    """Decode a 32-bit RISC-V RV32I instruction word."""
    inst = Instruction(raw=word)
    inst.opcode = word & 0x7F
    inst.rd     = (word >> 7) & 0x1F
    inst.funct3 = (word >> 12) & 0x7
    inst.rs1    = (word >> 15) & 0x1F
    inst.rs2    = (word >> 20) & 0x1F
    inst.funct7 = (word >> 25) & 0x7F

    if inst.opcode == OPCODE_R_TYPE:
        inst.fmt = 'R'
        inst.imm = 0

    elif inst.opcode in (OPCODE_I_TYPE, OPCODE_LOAD, OPCODE_JALR):
        inst.fmt = 'I'
        inst.imm = sign_extend((word >> 20) & 0xFFF, 12)

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_STORE:
        inst.fmt = 'S'
        imm_low = (word >> 7) & 0x1F
        imm_high = (word >> 25) & 0x7F
        inst.imm = sign_extend((imm_high << 5) | imm_low, 12)

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_BRANCH:
        inst.fmt = 'B'
        imm_11  = (word >> 7) & 0x1
        imm_4_1 = (word >> 8) & 0xF
        imm_10_5 = (word >> 25) & 0x3F
        imm_12  = (word >> 31) & 0x1
        raw_imm = (imm_12 << 12) | (imm_11 << 11) | (imm_10_5 << 5) | (imm_4_1 << 1)
        inst.imm = sign_extend(raw_imm, 13)

    elif inst.opcode in (OPCODE_LUI, OPCODE_AUIPC):
        inst.fmt = 'U'
        inst.imm = word & 0xFFFFF000

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_JAL:
        inst.fmt = 'J'
        imm_19_12 = (word >> 12) & 0xFF
        imm_11    = (word >> 20) & 0x1
        imm_10_1  = (word >> 21) & 0x3FF
        imm_20    = (word >> 31) & 0x1
        raw_imm = (imm_20 << 20) | (imm_19_12 << 12) | (imm_11 << 11) | (imm_10_1 << 1)
        inst.imm = sign_extend(raw_imm, 21)

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_SYSTEM:
        inst.fmt = 'I'
        inst.imm = sign_extend((word >> 20) & 0xFFF, 12)

    else:
        inst.fmt = '?'
        inst.imm = 0

    return inst


def execute(self, inst: Instruction) -> None:
    """Execute a decoded RV32I instruction."""
    next_pc = self.pc + 4

    if inst.opcode == OPCODE_R_TYPE:
        val1 = to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs1))
        val2 = to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs2))
        uval1 = self.regs.read(inst.rs1)
        uval2 = self.regs.read(inst.rs2)
        result = 0
        if inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_ADD_SUB:
            result = (val1 + val2) if inst.funct7 == 0 else (val1 - val2)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SLL:
            result = uval1 << (uval2 & 0x1F)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SLT:
            result = 1 if val1 < val2 else 0
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SLTU:
            result = 1 if uval1 < uval2 else 0
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_XOR:
            result = uval1 ^ uval2
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SRL_SRA:
            shamt = uval2 & 0x1F
            if inst.funct7 == 0b0100000:  # SRA
                result = val1 >> shamt
            else:  # SRL
                result = uval1 >> shamt
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_OR:
            result = uval1 | uval2
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_AND:
            result = uval1 & uval2
        self.regs.write(inst.rd, to_unsigned_32(result))

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_I_TYPE:
        val1 = to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs1))
        uval1 = self.regs.read(inst.rs1)
        result = 0
        if inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_ADD_SUB:  # ADDI
            result = val1 + inst.imm
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SLT:    # SLTI
            result = 1 if val1 < inst.imm else 0
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SLTU:   # SLTIU
            result = 1 if uval1 < to_unsigned_32(inst.imm) else 0
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_XOR:    # XORI
            result = uval1 ^ to_unsigned_32(inst.imm)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_OR:     # ORI
            result = uval1 | to_unsigned_32(inst.imm)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_AND:    # ANDI
            result = uval1 & to_unsigned_32(inst.imm)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SLL:    # SLLI
            shamt = inst.imm & 0x1F
            result = uval1 << shamt
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_SRL_SRA:
            shamt = inst.imm & 0x1F
            if inst.funct7 == 0b0100000:   # SRAI
                result = val1 >> shamt
            else:                          # SRLI
                result = uval1 >> shamt
        self.regs.write(inst.rd, to_unsigned_32(result))

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_LOAD:
        addr = to_unsigned_32(to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs1)) + inst.imm)
        if inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BYTE:     # LB
            val = self.memory.load_byte(addr)
            val = sign_extend(val, 8)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_HALF:   # LH
            val = self.memory.load_half(addr)
            val = sign_extend(val, 16)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_WORD:   # LW
            val = self.memory.load_word(addr)
            val = to_signed_32(val)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BYTE_U: # LBU
            val = self.memory.load_byte(addr)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_HALF_U: # LHU
            val = self.memory.load_half(addr)
        else:
            val = 0
        self.regs.write(inst.rd, to_unsigned_32(val))

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_STORE:
        addr = to_unsigned_32(to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs1)) + inst.imm)
        val = self.regs.read(inst.rs2)
        if inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BYTE:     # SB
            self.memory.store_byte(addr, val)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_HALF:   # SH
            self.memory.store_half(addr, val)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_WORD:   # SW
            self.memory.store_word(addr, val)

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_BRANCH:
        val1 = to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs1))
        val2 = to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs2))
        uval1 = self.regs.read(inst.rs1)
        uval2 = self.regs.read(inst.rs2)
        taken = False
        if inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BEQ:
            taken = (val1 == val2)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BNE:
            taken = (val1 != val2)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BLT:
            taken = (val1 < val2)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BGE:
            taken = (val1 >= val2)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BLTU:
            taken = (uval1 < uval2)
        elif inst.funct3 == FUNCT3_BGEU:
            taken = (uval1 >= uval2)
        if taken:
            next_pc = to_unsigned_32(self.pc + inst.imm)

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_JAL:
        self.regs.write(inst.rd, to_unsigned_32(self.pc + 4))
        next_pc = to_unsigned_32(self.pc + inst.imm)

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_JALR:
        target = to_unsigned_32(to_signed_32(self.regs.read(inst.rs1)) + inst.imm)
        target = target & ~1  # Clear least significant bit
        self.regs.write(inst.rd, to_unsigned_32(self.pc + 4))
        next_pc = target

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_LUI:
        self.regs.write(inst.rd, to_unsigned_32(inst.imm))

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_AUIPC:
        self.regs.write(inst.rd, to_unsigned_32(self.pc + inst.imm))

    elif inst.opcode == OPCODE_SYSTEM:
        self.halted = True  # ECALL used as halt

    self.pc = to_unsigned_32(next_pc)
milestones.testCode:
# --- Test: Instruction Decoding ---
# Test R-type: ADD x3, x1, x2 -> 0x002080B3
inst = decode(0x002080B3)
assert inst.fmt == 'R', f"Expected R-type, got {inst.fmt}"
assert inst.rd == 1, f"Expected rd=1, got {inst.rd}"  # Actually rd=1 in this encoding

# Test I-type: ADDI x1, x0, 10 -> 0x00A00093
inst = decode(0x00A00093)
assert inst.fmt == 'I', f"Expected I-type, got {inst.fmt}"
assert inst.imm == 10, f"Expected imm=10, got {inst.imm}"
assert inst.rd == 1, f"Expected rd=1, got {inst.rd}"

# --- Test: Fibonacci(10) ---
# Program computes fib(10) = 55 in x10
fib_program = [
    0x00100093,  # addi x1, x0, 1      # x1 = 1 (fib(1))
    0x00000113,  # addi x2, x0, 0      # x2 = 0 (fib(0))
    0x00A00193,  # addi x3, x0, 10     # x3 = 10 (counter)
    0x00100213,  # addi x4, x0, 1      # x4 = 1
    # loop:
    0x002082B3,  # add x5, x1, x2      # x5 = x1 + x2
    0x00008113,  # addi x2, x1, 0      # x2 = x1 (mv)
    0x00028093,  # addi x1, x5, 0      # x1 = x5 (mv)
    0xFFF18193,  # addi x3, x3, -1     # x3 -= 1
    0xFE419463,  # bne x3, x4, -24     # if x3 != 1, goto loop
    0x00008513,  # addi x10, x1, 0     # x10 = x1 (result)
    0x00000073,  # ecall               # halt
]
mem = Memory()
mem.load_program(fib_program)
cpu = CPU(mem)
cpu.run()
assert cpu.regs.read(10) == 55, f"fib(10) should be 55, got {cpu.regs.read(10)}"
assert cpu.halted, "CPU should be halted after ecall"

# --- Test: Simple Arithmetic ---
arith_program = [
    0x01400093,  # addi x1, x0, 20     # x1 = 20
    0x00A00113,  # addi x2, x0, 10     # x2 = 10
    0x402081B3,  # sub x3, x1, x2      # x3 = 20 - 10 = 10
    0x0020C233,  # xor x4, x1, x2      # x4 = 20 ^ 10 = 30
    0x0020E2B3,  # or x5, x1, x2       # x5 = 20 | 10 = 30
    0x0020F333,  # and x6, x1, x2      # x6 = 20 & 10 = 0
    0x0020A3B3,  # slt x7, x1, x2      # x7 = (20 < 10) = 0
    0x0010A433,  # slt x8, x1, x1      # x8 = (20 < 20) = 0
    0x00000073,  # ecall
]
mem2 = Memory()
mem2.load_program(arith_program)
cpu2 = CPU(mem2)
cpu2.run()
assert cpu2.regs.read(3) == 10, f"SUB: expected 10, got {cpu2.regs.read(3)}"
assert cpu2.regs.read(4) == 30, f"XOR: expected 30, got {cpu2.regs.read(4)}"
assert cpu2.regs.read(5) == 30, f"OR: expected 30, got {cpu2.regs.read(5)}"
assert cpu2.regs.read(6) == 0, f"AND: expected 0, got {cpu2.regs.read(6)}"
assert cpu2.regs.read(7) == 0, f"SLT: expected 0, got {cpu2.regs.read(7)}"

# --- Test: Load/Store ---
ls_program = [
    0x02A00093,  # addi x1, x0, 42     # x1 = 42
    0x10000113,  # addi x2, x0, 256    # x2 = 256 (base addr)
    0x00112023,  # sw x1, 0(x2)        # mem[256] = 42
    0x00012183,  # lw x3, 0(x2)        # x3 = mem[256] = 42
    0x00000073,  # ecall
]
mem3 = Memory()
mem3.load_program(ls_program)
cpu3 = CPU(mem3)
cpu3.run()
assert cpu3.regs.read(3) == 42, f"LW: expected 42, got {cpu3.regs.read(3)}"

# --- Test: CPI for single-cycle ---

milestones.title: 5-Stage Pipeline with Hazard Detection
milestones.description: Transform the single-cycle processor into a 5-stage pipelined processor with data forwarding and hazard detection. The classic IF-ID-EX-MEM-WB pipeline is the beating heart of every modern processor. You will implement forwarding paths to resolve data hazards without stalling whenever possible, and insert stalls only when truly necessary (load-use hazards).
learningObjectives: Understand the 5-stage pipeline: IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB
Implement pipeline registers to hold state between stages
Detect data hazards between consecutive instructions
Implement EX-EX and MEM-EX forwarding paths
Handle load-use hazards with pipeline stalls (bubbles)
Compute CPI for pipelined execution and understand pipeline overhead

@dataclass
class IF_ID_Register:
    """Pipeline register between Instruction Fetch and Instruction Decode."""
    pc: int = 0
    instruction_word: int = 0
    valid: bool = False

    def flush(self) -> None:
        """Invalidate this pipeline register (insert bubble)."""
        self.valid = False
        self.instruction_word = 0
        self.pc = 0


@dataclass
class ID_EX_Register:
    """Pipeline register between Instruction Decode and Execute."""
    pc: int = 0
    instruction: Optional[Instruction] = None
    read_data1: int = 0          # Value from rs1
    read_data2: int = 0          # Value from rs2
    rs1: int = 0                 # Source register 1 number
    rs2: int = 0                 # Source register 2 number
    rd: int = 0                  # Destination register number
    imm: int = 0                 # Sign-extended immediate
    opcode: int = 0
    funct3: int = 0
    funct7: int = 0
    reg_write: bool = False      # Will this instruction write a register?
    mem_read: bool = False       # Is this a load instruction?
    mem_write: bool = False      # Is this a store instruction?
    is_branch: bool = False      # Is this a branch/jump?
    valid: bool = False

    def flush(self) -> None:
        self.valid = False
        self.instruction = None
        self.rd = 0
        self.reg_write = False
        self.mem_read = False
        self.mem_write = False
        self.is_branch = False


@dataclass
class EX_MEM_Register:
    """Pipeline register between Execute and Memory."""
    pc: int = 0
    alu_result: int = 0          # Output of ALU
    write_data: int = 0          # Data to write to memory (for stores)
    rd: int = 0
    funct3: int = 0
    opcode: int = 0
    reg_write: bool = False
    mem_read: bool = False
    mem_write: bool = False
    branch_taken: bool = False
    branch_target: int = 0
    valid: bool = False

    def flush(self) -> None:
        self.valid = False
        self.rd = 0
        self.reg_write = False
        self.mem_read = False
        self.mem_write = False
        self.branch_taken = False


@dataclass
class MEM_WB_Register:
    """Pipeline register between Memory and Write Back."""
    alu_result: int = 0          # ALU result (pass-through)
    mem_data: int = 0            # Data loaded from memory
    rd: int = 0
    opcode: int = 0
    funct3: int = 0
    reg_write: bool = False
    mem_read: bool = False       # Was this a load? (selects mem_data vs alu_result)
    valid: bool = False

    def flush(self) -> None:
        self.valid = False
        self.rd = 0
        self.reg_write = False
        self.mem_read = False


@dataclass
class PipelineStats:
    """Track pipeline performance metrics."""
    total_cycles: int = 0
    instructions_completed: int = 0
    stalls_inserted: int = 0
    forwards_ex_ex: int = 0      # EX → EX forwarding events
    forwards_mem_ex: int = 0     # MEM → EX forwarding events
    branches_taken: int = 0
    branch_flushes: int = 0      # Cycles lost to branch misprediction

    @property
    def cpi(self) -> float:
        if self.instructions_completed == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.total_cycles / self.instructions_completed

    def report(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"=== Pipeline Performance ==="
            f"\n  Total cycles:         {self.total_cycles}"
            f"\n  Instructions:         {self.instructions_completed}"
            f"\n  CPI:                  {self.cpi:.3f}"
            f"\n  Stalls (load-use):    {self.stalls_inserted}"
            f"\n  Forwards (EX→EX):    {self.forwards_ex_ex}"
            f"\n  Forwards (MEM→EX):   {self.forwards_mem_ex}"
            f"\n  Branch flushes:       {self.branch_flushes}"

milestones.solutionCode:
# ForwardingUnit
@staticmethod
def get_forward_a(id_ex, ex_mem, mem_wb):
    if (ex_mem.valid and ex_mem.reg_write and ex_mem.rd != 0
            and ex_mem.rd == id_ex.rs1):
        return 1  # Forward from EX/MEM
    if (mem_wb.valid and mem_wb.reg_write and mem_wb.rd != 0
            and mem_wb.rd == id_ex.rs1):
        return 2  # Forward from MEM/WB
    return 0  # No forwarding

@staticmethod
def get_forward_b(id_ex, ex_mem, mem_wb):
    if (ex_mem.valid and ex_mem.reg_write and ex_mem.rd != 0
            and ex_mem.rd == id_ex.rs2):
        return 1
    if (mem_wb.valid and mem_wb.reg_write and mem_wb.rd != 0
            and mem_wb.rd == id_ex.rs2):
        return 2
    return 0

# HazardDetectionUnit
@staticmethod
def detect_load_use_hazard(id_ex, if_id_rs1, if_id_rs2):
    if id_ex.valid and id_ex.mem_read and id_ex.rd != 0:
        if id_ex.rd == if_id_rs1 or id_ex.rd == if_id_rs2:
            return True
    return False

# PipelinedCPU stages: WB, MEM, EX, ID, IF, and cycle orchestration
# See full solution in solutions/ directory.

# --- Test: Pipeline forwarding ---
# Program: x1=5, x2=10, x3=x1+x2 (needs forwarding), x4=x3+x1 (chain)
fwd_program = [
    0x00500093,  # addi x1, x0, 5
    0x00A00113,  # addi x2, x0, 10
    0x002081B3,  # add x3, x1, x2     # Needs forwarding from x1, x2
    0x001181B3,  # add x3, x3, x1     # Needs forwarding from x3
    0x00018213,  # addi x4, x3, 0     # Needs forwarding from x3
    0x00000073,  # ecall
]
mem = Memory()
mem.load_program(fwd_program)
pipe = PipelinedCPU(mem)
stats = pipe.run()
assert pipe.regs.read(1) == 5, f"x1 should be 5, got {pipe.regs.read(1)}"
assert pipe.regs.read(2) == 10, f"x2 should be 10, got {pipe.regs.read(2)}"
assert pipe.regs.read(3) == 20, f"x3 should be 20 (5+10+5), got {pipe.regs.read(3)}"
assert pipe.regs.read(4) == 20, f"x4 should be 20, got {pipe.regs.read(4)}"
assert stats.forwards_ex_ex > 0, "Should have EX-EX forwarding events"

# --- Test: Load-use hazard stall ---
# addi x1, x0, 42; sw x1, 0(x0); lw x2, 0(x0); add x3, x2, x1
# The add depends on the lw result → must stall one cycle
load_use_program = [
    0x02A00093,  # addi x1, x0, 42
    0x00102023,  # sw x1, 0(x0)       # NOTE: stores to addr 0 (overwriting instructions — that's fine, we already fetched them)
    0x10000293,  # addi x5, x0, 256   # base address in safe region
    0x00102023,  # sw x1, 0(x0)       # store 42
    0x0012A023,  # sw x1, 0(x5)       # store 42 at addr 256
    0x0002A103,  # lw x2, 0(x5)       # load from addr 256: x2 = 42
    0x001101B3,  # add x3, x2, x1     # x3 = x2 + x1 = 42 + 42 = 84 (LOAD-USE HAZARD)
    0x00000073,  # ecall
]
mem2 = Memory()
mem2.load_program(load_use_program)
pipe2 = PipelinedCPU(mem2)
stats2 = pipe2.run()
assert pipe2.regs.read(2) == 42, f"x2 should be 42, got {pipe2.regs.read(2)}"
assert pipe2.regs.read(3) == 84, f"x3 should be 84, got {pipe2.regs.read(3)}"
assert stats2.stalls_inserted > 0, "Should have at least one load-use stall"

# --- Test: CPI is greater than 1.0 (pipelining overhead) ---
assert stats2.cpi > 1.0, f"Pipelined CPI should be > 1.0 with stalls, got {stats2.cpi:.3f}"

# --- Test: Branch with pipeline ---
branch_program = [
    0x00500093,  # addi x1, x0, 5
    0x00300113,  # addi x2, x0, 3
    0x00208463,  # beq x1, x2, +8      # Not taken (5 != 3)
    0x00100193,  # addi x3, x0, 1      # x3 = 1 (should execute)
    0x00000073,  # ecall
]
mem3 = Memory()
mem3.load_program(branch_program)
pipe3 = PipelinedCPU(mem3)
pipe3.run()
assert pipe3.regs.read(3) == 1, f"x3 should be 1 (branch not taken), got {pipe3.regs.read(3)}"

# --- Test: Loop with pipeline ---
# Sum 1..5 = 15
loop_program = [
    0x00000093,  # addi x1, x0, 0      # sum = 0
    0x00100113,  # addi x2, x0, 1      # i = 1
    0x00500193,  # addi x3, x0, 5      # limit = 5
    # loop:
    0x002080B3,  # add x1, x1, x2      # sum += i
    0x00110113,  # addi x2, x2, 1      # i++
    0xFE315CE3,  # bge x2, x3, -8 ... actually let's use bne
]
# Simpler loop version
loop_program2 = [
    0x00000093,  # addi x1, x0, 0      # sum = 0
    0x00100113,  # addi x2, x0, 1      # i = 1
    0x00600193,  # addi x3, x0, 6      # limit = 6 (loop while i != 6)
    # loop:
    0x002080B3,  # add x1, x1, x2      # sum += i
    0x00110113,  # addi x2, x2, 1      # i++
    0xFE311CE3,  # bne x2, x3, loop    # if i != 6, loop back
    0x00000073,  # ecall
]
mem4 = Memory()
mem4.load_program(loop_program2)
pipe4 = PipelinedCPU(mem4)
stats4 = pipe4.run()
assert pipe4.regs.read(1) == 15, f"Sum 1..5 should be 15, got {pipe4.regs.read(1)}"
assert stats4.total_cycles > stats4.instructions_completed, "Cycles should exceed instructions due to pipeline overhead"

print('All Milestone 2 tests passed!')
hints: Implement pipeline stages in reverse order (WB first, then MEM, EX, ID, IF) so that earlier stages read the OLD values of later pipeline registers before they get updated.
The forwarding unit checks are straightforward conditions — draw the datapath diagram with forwarding mux connections before coding.
For load-use hazards, you need exactly ONE stall cycle. During the stall: keep PC the same, keep IF/ID the same, and insert a NOP (bubble) into ID/EX.
Branch resolution happens in the EX stage. When a branch is taken, flush IF/ID and ID/EX to cancel the two wrongly-fetched instructions.
Debug tip: add a trace mode that prints the contents of all pipeline registers each cycle. This makes hazard debugging much easier.

milestones.title: Configurable L1 Cache
milestones.description: Implement a configurable L1 data cache supporting both direct-mapped and set-associative organizations with LRU replacement. Integrate it into the memory stage of your pipeline and measure how cache configuration affects program performance through hit rates and average memory access time (AMAT).
learningObjectives: Understand cache organization: sets, ways, blocks, tags
Implement direct-mapped and N-way set-associative caches
Build an LRU replacement policy using access timestamps
Calculate tag, index, and offset from a memory address
Measure hit rate, miss rate, and AMAT
Analyze how access patterns interact with cache configuration

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, List, Dict, Tuple
import math

# =============================================================================
# RISC-V Processor — Milestone 3
# Configurable L1 Data Cache
# =============================================================================


@dataclass
class CacheConfig:
    """Configuration for an L1 cache.
    
    Attributes:
        cache_size:     Total cache size in bytes (e.g., 1024, 4096)
        block_size:     Size of each cache block/line in bytes (e.g., 16, 32, 64)
        associativity:  Number of ways (1 = direct-mapped, 4 = 4-way, etc.)
        miss_penalty:   Cycles to service a cache miss (e.g., 10, 50, 100)
        hit_time:       Cycles for a cache hit (typically 1)
    """
    cache_size: int = 4096        # 4 KB
    block_size: int = 32          # 32 bytes per block
    associativity: int = 4        # 4-way set associative
    miss_penalty: int = 100       # 100 cycles on miss
    hit_time: int = 1             # 1 cycle on hit

    @property
    def num_blocks(self) -> int:
        """Total number of cache blocks."""
        return self.cache_size // self.block_size

    @property
    def num_sets(self) -> int:
        """Number of sets in the cache."""
        return self.num_blocks // self.associativity

    @property
    def offset_bits(self) -> int:
        """Number of bits for block offset."""
        return int(math.log2(self.block_size))

    @property
    def index_bits(self) -> int:
        """Number of bits for set index."""
        return int(math.log2(self.num_sets))

    @property
    def tag_bits(self) -> int:
        """Number of bits for tag."""
        return 32 - self.offset_bits - self.index_bits

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        return (f"CacheConfig({self.cache_size}B, {self.block_size}B blocks, "
                f"{self.associativity}-way, {self.num_sets} sets, "
                f"miss_penalty={self.miss_penalty})")


@dataclass
class CacheLine:
    """A single cache line (block) within a set.
    
    Attributes:
        valid:          Whether this line contains valid data
        tag:            Tag portion of the address
        data:           The cached block of data (list of bytes)
        last_access:    Timestamp of last access (for LRU)
        dirty:          Whether this line has been written to (for write-back)
    """
    valid: bool = False
    tag: int = 0
    data: List[int] = field(default_factory=list)
    last_access: int = 0
    dirty: bool = False


@dataclass
class CacheStats:
    """Cache performance statistics."""
    hits: int = 0
    misses: int = 0
    evictions: int = 0
    total_accesses: int = 0

    @property
    def hit_rate(self) -> float:
        if self.total_accesses == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.hits / self.total_accesses

    @property
    def miss_rate(self) -> float:
        if self.total_accesses == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.misses / self.total_accesses

    def amat(self, hit_time: int, miss_penalty: int) -> float:
        """Average Memory Access Time.
        AMAT = hit_time + miss_rate * miss_penalty
        """
        return hit_time + self.miss_rate * miss_penalty

    def report(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"=== Cache Statistics ==="
            f"\n  Total accesses:  {self.total_accesses}"
            f"\n  Hits:            {self.hits}"
            f"\n  Misses:          {self.misses}"
            f"\n  Hit rate:        {self.hit_rate:.4f}"
            f"\n  Miss rate:       {self.miss_rate:.4f}"
            f"\n  Evictions:       {self.evictions}"
        )


class Cache:
    """Configurable L1 cache with LRU replacement.
    
    Supports direct-mapped (associativity=1) and N-way set-associative.

    def read_word(self, addr: int) -> int:
        """Read a 32-bit word (4 bytes, little-endian) from cache."""
        b0 = self.read(addr)
        b1 = self.read(addr + 1)
        b2 = self.read(addr + 2)
        b3 = self.read(addr + 3)
        return b0 | (b1 << 8) | (b2 << 16) | (b3 << 24)

    def write_word(self, addr: int, value: int) -> None:
        """Write a 32-bit word (4 bytes, little-endian) to cache."""
        self.write(addr, value & 0xFF)
        self.write(addr + 1, (value >> 8) & 0xFF)
        self.write(addr + 2, (value >> 16) & 0xFF)
        self.write(addr + 3, (value >> 24) & 0xFF)

    def flush_all(self) -> None:
        """Write back all dirty lines to backing memory."""
        for cache_set in self.sets:
            for line in cache_set:
                if line.valid and line.dirty:
                    # Write back (students can implement if desired)
                    line.dirty = False

    def get_amat(self) -> float:
        """Get the Average Memory Access Time."""
        return self.stats.amat(self.config.hit_time, self.config.miss_penalty)

config = CacheConfig(cache_size=1024, block_size=32, associativity=4)
mem = Memory()
cache = Cache(config, mem)

# With 1024B, 32B blocks, 4-way: 8 blocks total, 2 sets
# offset: 5 bits, index: 1 bit, tag: 26 bits
tag, idx, off = cache._decompose_address(0x00000000)
assert off == 0, f"Offset should be 0, got {off}"
assert idx == 0, f"Index should be 0, got {idx}"

tag2, idx2, off2 = cache._decompose_address(0x00000025)  # offset=5, index=1
assert off2 == 5, f"Offset should be 5, got {off2}"
assert idx2 == 1, f"Index should be 1, got {idx2}"

# --- Test: Sequential access pattern (high hit rate) ---
config_seq = CacheConfig(cache_size=4096, block_size=64, associativity=4, miss_penalty=100)
mem_seq = Memory()
# Write sequential data
for i in range(256):
    mem_seq.store_byte(i, i & 0xFF)
cache_seq = Cache(config_seq, mem_seq)

# Read 256 bytes sequentially, then read again — second pass should be all hits
for i in range(256):
    val = cache_seq.read(i)
    assert val == (i & 0xFF), f"Read mismatch at addr {i}"

first_pass_misses = cache_seq.stats.misses

for i in range(256):
    val = cache_seq.read(i)
    assert val == (i & 0xFF), f"Re-read mismatch at addr {i}"

second_pass_new_misses = cache_seq.stats.misses - first_pass_misses
assert second_pass_new_misses == 0, f"Second pass should have 0 new misses, got {second_pass_new_misses}"
assert cache_seq.stats.hit_rate > 0.5, f"Overall hit rate should be > 0.5, got {cache_seq.stats.hit_rate:.4f}"

# --- Test: Strided access (predictable misses) ---
config_stride = CacheConfig(cache_size=1024, block_size=32, associativity=1, miss_penalty=100)
mem_stride = Memory()
for i in range(0, 2048, 4):
    mem_stride.store_word(i, i)
cache_stride = Cache(config_stride, mem_stride)

# Access every 64 bytes (stride = 2 * block_size) — every access misses differently
for i in range(0, 2048, 64):
    cache_stride.read(i)

assert cache_stride.stats.misses > 0, "Strided access should have misses"
assert cache_stride.stats.total_accesses == 32, f"Should have 32 accesses, got {cache_stride.stats.total_accesses}"

# --- Test: AMAT calculation ---
amat = cache_seq.get_amat()
assert amat >= 1.0, f"AMAT should be >= 1.0 (hit_time), got {amat}"
assert amat < 100.0, f"AMAT should be much less than miss_penalty for sequential, got {amat}"

# --- Test: Write and read back ---
config_wr = CacheConfig(cache_size=4096, block_size=32, associativity=4)
mem_wr = Memory()
cache_wr = Cache(config_wr, mem_wr)
cache_wr.write(100, 0xAB)
result = cache_wr.read(100)
assert result == 0xAB, f"Write-read: expected 0xAB, got {result:#x}"

# --- Test: LRU eviction ---
# Direct-mapped cache with 2 sets, 1 way — address aliasing forces eviction
config_dm = CacheConfig(cache_size=64, block_size=32, associativity=1, miss_penalty=10)
mem_dm = Memory()
mem_dm.store_byte(0, 0x11)
mem_dm.store_byte(64, 0x22)   # Maps to same set as addr 0
mem_dm.store_byte(128, 0x33)  # Also same set
cache_dm = Cache(config_dm, mem_dm)

cache_dm.read(0)    # Miss, load block
cache_dm.read(64)   # Miss, evict addr 0's block
cache_dm.read(0)    # Miss again — was evicted

assert cache_dm.stats.misses == 3, f"Should have 3 misses due to conflicts, got {cache_dm.stats.misses}"
assert cache_dm.stats.evictions >= 1, f"Should have evictions, got {cache_dm.stats.evictions}"

For LRU, use a simple timestamp approach: each line has a last_access counter. On access, set it to a global monotonic counter. When evicting, pick the line with the smallest last_access.
Direct-mapped is just set-associative with associativity=1. If your set-associative code is correct, direct-mapped works automatically.
For write-back: on eviction of a dirty line, write its data back to the backing memory before loading the new block.
Test with small caches first (e.g., 64 bytes, 16-byte blocks) so you can trace every access by hand to verify correctness.

class PredictionState(IntEnum):
    """2-bit saturating counter states.
    
    State machine:
      STRONGLY_NOT_TAKEN (0) <-> WEAKLY_NOT_TAKEN (1) <-> WEAKLY_TAKEN (2) <-> STRONGLY_TAKEN (3)
    
    Predict taken if state >= 2, not taken if state < 2.
    On correct prediction: move toward the extreme (0 or 3).
    On misprediction: move one step toward the other extreme.
    """
    STRONGLY_NOT_TAKEN = 0
    WEAKLY_NOT_TAKEN = 1
    WEAKLY_TAKEN = 2
    STRONGLY_TAKEN = 3

@dataclass

    counter: SaturatingCounter = field(default_factory=SaturatingCounter)

@dataclass
class BranchPredictorStats:
    """Branch prediction performance metrics."""
    total_branches: int = 0
    correct_predictions: int = 0
    mispredictions: int = 0
    taken_predicted_taken: int = 0       # True positive
    taken_predicted_not_taken: int = 0   # False negative
    not_taken_predicted_taken: int = 0   # False positive
    not_taken_predicted_not_taken: int = 0  # True negative

    @property
    def accuracy(self) -> float:
        if self.total_branches == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.correct_predictions / self.total_branches

    @property
    def misprediction_rate(self) -> float:
        if self.total_branches == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.mispredictions / self.total_branches

    def report(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"=== Branch Predictor Statistics ==="
            f"\n  Total branches:          {self.total_branches}"
            f"\n  Correct predictions:     {self.correct_predictions}"
            f"\n  Mispredictions:          {self.mispredictions}"
            f"\n  Accuracy:                {self.accuracy:.4f}"
            f"\n  Misprediction rate:      {self.misprediction_rate:.4f}"
            f"\n  --- Confusion Matrix ---"
            f"\n  Taken & predicted taken:      {self.taken_predicted_taken}"
            f"\n  Taken & predicted not-taken:  {self.taken_predicted_not_taken}"
            f"\n  Not-taken & predicted taken:  {self.not_taken_predicted_taken}"
            f"\n  Not-taken & predicted NT:     {self.not_taken_predicted_not_taken}"
        )

# --- Test: Saturating counter basics ---
c = SaturatingCounter(state=PredictionState.WEAKLY_NOT_TAKEN)
assert c.predict_taken() == False, "WNT should predict not taken"
c.update(True)   # -> WEAKLY_TAKEN
assert c.state == PredictionState.WEAKLY_TAKEN
assert c.predict_taken() == True, "WT should predict taken"
c.update(True)   # -> STRONGLY_TAKEN
assert c.state == PredictionState.STRONGLY_TAKEN
c.update(True)   # saturate at STRONGLY_TAKEN
assert c.state == PredictionState.STRONGLY_TAKEN, "Should saturate at ST"
c.update(False)  # -> WEAKLY_TAKEN
assert c.state == PredictionState.WEAKLY_TAKEN

# --- Test: BHT index mapping ---
bp = BranchPredictor(bht_size=64)
assert bp._get_index(0x00000000) == 0, "PC=0 should map to index 0"
assert bp._get_index(0x00000004) == 1, "PC=4 should map to index 1"
assert bp._get_index(0x00000100) == 0, "PC=256 should wrap: (256>>2)%64 = 0"

# --- Test: Loop prediction accuracy (should be >90%) ---
# Simulate a simple loop that runs 100 iterations: branch taken 99 times, not taken once
bp_loop = BranchPredictor(bht_size=128)
loop_pc = 0x00001000  # Branch instruction PC
loop_target = 0x00000FE0  # Loop body start
for i in range(100):
    taken = (i < 99)  # Taken for iterations 0-98, not taken on last
    target = loop_target if taken else loop_pc + 4
    bp_loop.predict(loop_pc)
    bp_loop.update(loop_pc, taken, target)

assert bp_loop.stats.accuracy > 0.90, f"Loop accuracy should be >90%, got {bp_loop.stats.accuracy:.2%}"
assert bp_loop.stats.total_branches == 100

# --- Test: Alternating branches (lower accuracy) ---
# Branch alternates taken/not-taken — worst case for 2-bit predictor
bp_alt = BranchPredictor(bht_size=128)
alt_pc = 0x00002000
for i in range(100):
    taken = (i % 2 == 0)
    bp_alt.predict(alt_pc)
    bp_alt.update(alt_pc, taken, alt_pc - 8 if taken else alt_pc + 4)

assert bp_alt.stats.accuracy < 0.60, f"Alternating accuracy should be <60%, got {bp_alt.stats.accuracy:.2%}"

# --- Test: Nested loop prediction ---
bp_nested = BranchPredictor(bht_size=256)
outer_pc = 0x00003000
inner_pc = 0x00003010
for outer in range(10):
    for inner in range(20):
        inner_taken = (inner < 19)
        bp_nested.predict(inner_pc)
        bp_nested.update(inner_pc, inner_taken, inner_pc - 16 if inner_taken else inner_pc + 4)
    outer_taken = (outer < 9)
    bp_nested.predict(outer_pc)
    bp_nested.update(outer_pc, outer_taken, outer_pc - 48 if outer_taken else outer_pc + 4)

assert bp_nested.stats.accuracy > 0.85, f"Nested loop accuracy should be >85%, got {bp_nested.stats.accuracy:.2%}"
assert bp_nested.stats.total_branches == 210  # 200 inner + 10 outer

# --- Test: Confusion matrix consistency ---
assert (bp_loop.stats.taken_predicted_taken + bp_loop.stats.taken_predicted_not_taken +
        bp_loop.stats.not_taken_predicted_taken + bp_loop.stats.not_taken_predicted_not_taken) == 100, \
    "Confusion matrix should sum to total branches"

hints: The 2-bit saturating counter is a simple state machine with 4 states (0-3). Increment on taken, decrement on not taken, and clamp to [0, 3].
Index the BHT using (PC >> 2) % bht_size. Shifting right by 2 removes the two LSBs (always 0 for aligned instructions).
Store the prediction result (taken/not-taken, target) in the IF/ID pipeline register so it can be compared against the actual outcome in the EX stage.
On misprediction, flush both IF/ID and ID/EX and set PC to the correct target. This costs 2 cycles — you were speculatively fetching the wrong instructions.
The 2-bit counter tolerates a single anomaly before switching prediction. That is why loops (many consecutive takens) work well, but alternating patterns do not.

@dataclass
class ProcessorConfig:
    """Complete processor configuration for benchmarking."""
    name: str
    description: str
    pipeline_enabled: bool = True
    cache_enabled: bool = False
    cache_config: Optional['CacheConfig'] = None
    bp_enabled: bool = False
    bp_size: int = 256

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        parts = [self.name]
        if self.pipeline_enabled:
            parts.append("pipelined")
        else:
            parts.append("single-cycle")
        if self.cache_enabled and self.cache_config:
            parts.append(f"cache({self.cache_config.cache_size}B, "
                        f"{self.cache_config.associativity}-way)")
        if self.bp_enabled:
            parts.append(f"bp({self.bp_size}-entry)")
        return " | ".join(parts)


@dataclass
class BenchmarkResult:
    """Results from running a single benchmark on a single configuration."""
    config_name: str
    benchmark_name: str
    total_cycles: int = 0
    instructions_completed: int = 0
    cpi: float = 0.0
    stalls: int = 0
    forwards: int = 0
    cache_hits: int = 0
    cache_misses: int = 0
    cache_hit_rate: float = 0.0
    cache_amat: float = 0.0
    bp_predictions: int = 0
    bp_correct: int = 0
    bp_accuracy: float = 0.0
    branch_flushes: int = 0
    execution_correct: bool = False    # Did the benchmark produce correct output?

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"  Config: {self.config_name}",
            f"  Benchmark: {self.benchmark_name}",
            f"  Cycles: {self.total_cycles}  |  Instructions: {self.instructions_completed}  |  CPI: {self.cpi:.3f}",
        ]
        if self.cache_hits + self.cache_misses > 0:
            lines.append(f"  Cache: {self.cache_hit_rate:.2%} hit rate  |  AMAT: {self.cache_amat:.2f} cycles")
        if self.bp_predictions > 0:
            lines.append(f"  Branch predictor: {self.bp_accuracy:.2%} accuracy  |  Flushes: {self.branch_flushes}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

    name: str

    expected_registers: Dict[int, int]  # Expected register values after execution
    expected_memory: Dict[int, int]     # Expected memory values after execution

    def validate(self, regs: 'RegisterFile', memory: 'Memory') -> bool:
        """Check if execution produced correct results."""
        for reg, expected in self.expected_registers.items():
            actual = regs.read(reg)
            if actual != expected:
                return False
        for addr, expected in self.expected_memory.items():
            actual = memory.load_word(addr)
            if actual != expected:

        return True

milestones.solutionCode:
# get_default_configs
def get_default_configs():
    from milestone3 import CacheConfig
    return [
        ProcessorConfig(name='single-cycle', description='Baseline', pipeline_enabled=False),
        ProcessorConfig(name='pipeline-only', description='5-stage pipeline', pipeline_enabled=True),
        ProcessorConfig(name='pipeline+dm-cache', description='Pipeline + direct-mapped 1KB cache',
                       pipeline_enabled=True, cache_enabled=True,
                       cache_config=CacheConfig(cache_size=1024, block_size=32, associativity=1, miss_penalty=100)),
        ProcessorConfig(name='pipeline+4way-cache', description='Pipeline + 4-way 4KB cache',
                       pipeline_enabled=True, cache_enabled=True,
                       cache_config=CacheConfig(cache_size=4096, block_size=64, associativity=4, miss_penalty=100)),
        ProcessorConfig(name='pipeline+bp', description='Pipeline + 256-entry branch predictor',
                       pipeline_enabled=True, bp_enabled=True, bp_size=256),
        ProcessorConfig(name='full', description='Pipeline + 4-way cache + branch predictor',
                       pipeline_enabled=True, cache_enabled=True,
                       cache_config=CacheConfig(cache_size=4096, block_size=64, associativity=4, miss_penalty=100),
                       bp_enabled=True, bp_size=256),
    ]

# compare_cpi
def compare_cpi(results):
    matrix = {}
    for r in results:
        if r.benchmark_name not in matrix:
            matrix[r.benchmark_name] = {}
        matrix[r.benchmark_name][r.config_name] = r.cpi
    return matrix

# BenchmarkRunner methods follow standard patterns:
# _prepare_memory loads program and data, _create_processor instantiates
# based on config flags, run_single collects stats into BenchmarkResult,
# generate_report formats all results with analysis.

# --- Test: Configurations are valid ---
configs = get_default_configs()
assert len(configs) >= 6, f"Should have at least 6 configurations, got {len(configs)}"

config_names = [c.name for c in configs]
assert 'single-cycle' in config_names or any('single' in n for n in config_names), \
    "Should have a single-cycle baseline config"
assert any('full' in n or ('cache' in n and 'bp' in n) for n in config_names), \
    "Should have a full config with cache + BP"

# --- Test: Benchmark validation ---
# Create a simple benchmark and verify it runs
from milestone1 import Memory, CPU
simple_bench = Benchmark(
    name='simple_add',
    description='Add two numbers',
    program=[
        0x00500093,  # addi x1, x0, 5
        0x00A00113,  # addi x2, x0, 10
        0x002081B3,  # add x3, x1, x2
        0x00000073,  # ecall
    ],
    data={},
    expected_registers={3: 15},
    expected_memory={}
)
mem = Memory()
mem.load_program(simple_bench.program)
cpu = CPU(mem)
cpu.run()
assert simple_bench.validate(cpu.regs, mem), "Simple benchmark should validate correctly"

# --- Test: CPI comparison matrix ---
mock_results = [
    BenchmarkResult(config_name='single-cycle', benchmark_name='fibonacci', cpi=1.0, total_cycles=100, instructions_completed=100),
    BenchmarkResult(config_name='pipeline', benchmark_name='fibonacci', cpi=1.4, total_cycles=140, instructions_completed=100),
    BenchmarkResult(config_name='single-cycle', benchmark_name='bubblesort', cpi=1.0, total_cycles=500, instructions_completed=500),
    BenchmarkResult(config_name='pipeline', benchmark_name='bubblesort', cpi=1.3, total_cycles=650, instructions_completed=500),
]
cpi_matrix = compare_cpi(mock_results)
assert 'fibonacci' in cpi_matrix, "CPI matrix should have fibonacci benchmark"
assert 'bubblesort' in cpi_matrix, "CPI matrix should have bubblesort benchmark"
assert cpi_matrix['fibonacci']['single-cycle'] == 1.0, "Single-cycle CPI should be 1.0"
assert cpi_matrix['fibonacci']['pipeline'] == 1.4, "Pipeline CPI should match"

# --- Test: BenchmarkResult summary ---
result = BenchmarkResult(
    config_name='test', benchmark_name='test_bench',
    total_cycles=200, instructions_completed=150, cpi=1.333,
    cache_hits=80, cache_misses=20, cache_hit_rate=0.8, cache_amat=21.0,
    bp_predictions=30, bp_correct=27, bp_accuracy=0.9, branch_flushes=3
)
summary = result.summary()
assert 'CPI' in summary, "Summary should include CPI"
assert 'hit rate' in summary.lower() or 'cache' in summary.lower(), "Summary should include cache info"

# --- Test: ProcessorConfig representation ---
for config in configs:
    repr_str = repr(config)
    assert len(repr_str) > 0, f"Config {config.name} should have non-empty repr"

print('All Milestone 5 tests passed!')

In generate_report, structure the output clearly: one section per analysis dimension (CPI, cache, BP). Use tables or aligned text for readability.

CPI for single-cycle is always 1.0 by definition. Pipeline CPI > 1.0 due to hazards. Cache and BP reduce the gap but never eliminate it entirely.

testPrograms.name: Fibonacci
testPrograms.description: Compute fib(20) = 6765 recursively. Heavy branching with the loop branch taken 18 times and not taken once. Tests branch prediction accuracy and basic arithmetic.

instructions.comment: addi x1, x0, 1      # a = 1 (fib(1))
addi x2, x0, 0      # b = 0 (fib(0))
addi x3, x0, 20     # n = 20 (iterations)
addi x4, x0, 1      # constant 1
add  x5, x1, x2     # temp = a + b
addi x2, x1, 0      # b = a
addi x1, x5, 0      # a = temp
addi x3, x3, -1     # n--
bne  x3, x4, -24    # if n != 1, loop (offset -24 = back 6 instructions)
addi x10, x1, 0     # result in x10
ecall                # halt

testPrograms.name: Bubble Sort
testPrograms.description: Sort an array of 100 integers using bubble sort. Nested loops with O(n^2) comparisons and O(n^2) swaps in worst case. Generates heavy memory traffic (loads and stores) and many branch decisions.

instructions.comment: addi x1, x0, 256    # x1 = base address of array (256)
addi x2, x0, 100    # x2 = array length
addi x3, x0, 0      # x3 = outer loop index i = 0
addi x4, x2, -1     # x4 = n - 1
beq  x3, x4, +8     # if i == n-1, done outer loop (jump to end)
addi x5, x0, 0      # x5 = inner loop index j = 0
sub  x28, x2, x3    # x28 = n - i
beq  x5, x28, +24   # if j == n-i-1, done inner loop
slli x6, x5, 2      # x6 = j * 4 (byte offset)
add  x7, x1, x6     # x7 = &array[j]
lw   x8, 0(x7)      # x8 = array[j]
lw   x9, 4(x7)      # x9 = array[j+1]
bge  x8, x9, +12    # if array[j] >= array[j+1], no swap needed — skip
sw   x9, 0(x7)      # array[j] = array[j+1]
sw   x8, 4(x7)      # array[j+1] = array[j]
addi x5, x5, 1      # j++
beq  x0, x0, -52    # unconditional jump back to inner loop check (j < n-i-1)
addi x3, x3, 1      # i++
beq  x0, x0, -84    # unconditional jump back to outer loop check (i < n-1)
ecall                # halt

expectedResult.description: Array at address 256 is sorted in ascending order

branchProfile.description: Approximately 5000+ branch decisions from nested loops

testPrograms.name: Matrix Multiply
testPrograms.description: Multiply two 8x8 matrices. Regular, strided access pattern that is friendly to caching when block size is large enough. Three nested loops with predictable branch behavior.

instructions.comment: addi x1, x0, 256    # x1 = base addr matrix A (256)
addi x2, x0, 512    # x2 = base addr matrix B (512)
addi x3, x0, 768    # x3 = base addr matrix C (768)
addi x4, x0, 8      # x4 = dimension N = 8
addi x5, x0, 0      # x5 = i = 0 (row)
beq  x5, x4, +8     # if i == N, done
addi x6, x0, 0      # x6 = j = 0 (col)
beq  x6, x4, +8     # if j == N, next row
addi x7, x0, 0      # x7 = k = 0
addi x28, x0, 0     # x28 = accumulator = 0
beq  x7, x4, +8     # if k == N, store result
slli x8, x5, 3      # x8 = i * 8
add  x8, x8, x7     # x8 = i*8 + k
slli x8, x8, 2      # x8 = (i*8+k) * 4 (byte offset)
add  x8, x8, x1     # x8 = &A[i][k]
slli x10, x7, 3     # x10 = k * 8
mul  x12, x9, x11   # x12 = A[i][k] * B[k][j]  -- NOTE: uses M extension or manual multiply
add  x28, x28, x12  # acc += product
addi x7, x7, 1      # k++
beq  x0, x0, -84    # jump back to k-loop check
slli x8, x5, 3      # x8 = i * 8
add  x8, x8, x6     # x8 = i*8 + j
add  x8, x8, x3     # x8 = &C[i][j]
sw   x28, 0(x8)     # C[i][j] = acc
addi x6, x6, 1      # j++
beq  x0, x0, -116   # jump back to j-loop check
addi x5, x5, 1      # i++
beq  x0, x0, -148   # jump back to i-loop check
ecall                # halt

expectedResult.description: Matrix C at address 768 contains the product A * B

branchProfile.description: Highly predictable: 3 nested loops with N=8, each loop branch taken N-1 times consecutively

testPrograms.name: Linked List Traversal
testPrograms.description: Traverse a linked list of 50 nodes and sum the values. Each node is 8 bytes: [value (4B), next_ptr (4B)]. Nodes are allocated at non-contiguous addresses to create a cache-unfriendly pointer-chasing pattern.

instructions.comment: addi x1, x0, 256    # x1 = address of first node (head pointer)
lw   x2, 0(x1)      # x2 = head pointer value (address of first node data)
addi x3, x0, 0      # x3 = sum = 0
beq  x2, x0, +8     # if current == null, done
lw   x4, 0(x2)      # x4 = current->value
add  x3, x4, x3     # sum += value (corrected: add x3, x4, x3)
lw   x2, 4(x2)      # x2 = current->next
beq  x0, x0, -20    # jump back to null check
addi x10, x3, 0     # result in x10
ecall                # halt

expectedResult.description: Sum of all node values in the linked list

branchProfile.description: Loop branch taken 49 times, not taken once. But pointer chasing creates data-dependent loads that cause cache misses.

testPrograms.memoryLayout: Nodes scattered at addresses: 512, 1024, 768, 1536, 2048, ... (stride varies per node to defeat prefetching)

testPrograms.name: Binary Search
testPrograms.description: Perform binary search for a target value in a sorted array of 1024 integers. Logarithmic access pattern (log2(1024) = 10 comparisons). Tests branch prediction on data-dependent branches and cache behavior on halving access patterns.

instructions.comment: addi x1, x0, 256    # x1 = base address of sorted array
addi x2, x0, 1024   # x2 = array length
addi x3, x0, 500    # x3 = search target (value 500)
addi x4, x0, 0      # x4 = low = 0
addi x5, x2, 0      # x5 = high = length
bge  x4, x5, +8     # if low >= high, not found — exit loop
add  x5, x4, x5     # x5 = low + high (temp, will restore)
srai x5, x5, 1      # x5 = mid = (low + high) / 2 (arithmetic right shift)
slli x6, x5, 2      # x6 = mid * 4 (byte offset)
add  x7, x1, x6     # x7 = &array[mid]
lw   x8, 0(x7)      # x8 = array[mid]
beq  x8, x3, +12    # if array[mid] == target, found!
blt  x9, x3, +8     # NOTE: compare array[mid] < target
addi x4, x5, 0      # low = mid (search right half)
addi x5, x5, 0      # (placeholder for high = mid)
beq  x0, x0, -52    # jump back to loop check
addi x10, x5, 0     # x10 = found index (mid)
ecall                # halt

expectedResult.description: Index of the target value in the sorted array, or -1 if not found

branchProfile.description: Approximately 10 iterations (log2 1024). Each branch (less-than comparison) is data-dependent and unpredictable — roughly 50% taken.

testPrograms.accessPattern: Accesses array[512], then array[256] or array[768], then further halving — spans entire array, poor temporal locality.

criteria.description: Instruction decoder correctly handles all 6 RV32I formats (R, I, S, B, U, J) with proper sign extension
Single-cycle simulator executes all RV32I instructions correctly: arithmetic, logical, shifts, loads/stores, branches, jumps
Pipeline produces identical results to single-cycle for all test programs (functional correctness preserved across microarchitectural changes)
Forwarding correctly resolves EX-EX and MEM-EX data hazards without stalling
Load-use hazard detection inserts exactly one stall cycle and produces correct results
Cache lookup, insertion, and eviction produce correct data for all access patterns
Branch predictor correctly updates state and produces accurate predictions for loop-heavy programs (>90% accuracy)
All test programs run to completion and produce correct outputs across all configurations
Clean separation of concerns: decoder, ALU, memory, control, forwarding, hazard detection are modular and independently testable
Effective use of Python dataclasses for pipeline registers, cache lines, and configuration objects
Consistent 32-bit value handling: proper masking, sign extension, and overflow semantics throughout
Cache design is truly configurable: changing CacheConfig parameters produces correct behavior without code changes
Pipeline stages are cleanly separated with well-defined interfaces between stages via pipeline registers
CPI measurements are accurate and consistent: single-cycle = 1.0, pipeline > 1.0, and differences are explainable
Cache analysis covers hit rates, miss rates, and AMAT across at least 3 access patterns (sequential, strided, random/pointer-chasing)
Branch prediction analysis demonstrates >90% accuracy on loops and explains why alternating patterns have lower accuracy
Meaningful comparison across configurations with data-driven conclusions about performance trade-offs
Analysis identifies and explains at least 3 specific performance insights (e.g., cache size vs. associativity trade-off, branch pattern sensitivity, interaction effects)
Code has clear docstrings explaining what each class and method does, including parameter descriptions and return values
Complex algorithms (immediate extraction, forwarding logic, LRU eviction) have inline comments explaining the 'why', not just the 'what'
Performance report is well-structured with clear tables, labeled metrics, and a conclusions section that synthesizes findings

bonusPoints.description: Implement a global branch history register (GHR) that XORs with PC for gshare-style prediction. Compare accuracy against basic BHT for correlated branches.
Add a 2-level cache hierarchy (L1 + L2) with inclusive policy. Analyze AMAT improvement for programs that exceed L1 capacity.
Implement a branch target buffer (BTB) separate from the BHT. Measure the reduction in misdirected fetches for indirect jumps.
